content.full_text:
2 FPPC OPINIONS 130

BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

A-76-08-020

In the Matter of:

Opinion requested by
Francis Willmarth,
Attorney Representing
California Democratic Council

)
)
)
)
)
)
)

No. 75-188
August 3, 1976

BY THE COMMISSION: We have been asked the following
question by Francis Willmarth, representing the California Democratic Council:

The California Democratic Council (hereafter referred
to as "CDC") is a nonprofit corporation whose members consist, in
part, of Democratic Clubs throughout the state. Neither the CDC
nor the member clubs are committees or subcommittees of the Democratic Party.

Individual Democrats who join Democratic Clubs customarily pay dues to the clubs ranging between $3.00 and $6.00 per
person per year. At the time the club collects these dues, it
normally does not know the exact use to which the funds will be
put. Approximately $2.00 per person is paid to affiliate the
individual member with CDC. The remaining portion of each member's dues is used for various purposes, including: (1) payments
to county central committees and to local club federations; (2)
payments to print and distribute the regular club newsletter to
members; and (3) payments to hold club meetings at which the
clubs may have programs on political and social matters of current interest.

The dues are not collected for the purpose of making
contributions to candidates or ballot measures. If a club decides to support a candidate or ballot measure, the normal practice is to hold special fund raising efforts to raise money for
that candidate or ballot measure.

On the basis of these facts, should the dues be counted
as "contributions" in determining whether a club has received
sufficient contributions to make it a committee within the meaning of the Political Reform Act?

No. 75-188
Page Two

2 FPPC OPINIONS 131

CONCLUSION

Dues received by the Democratic Clubs are not contri-
butions, unless the dues are "'earmarked' for the making of con-
tributions or expenditures." A payment is "earmarked" when the
donor knows or has reason to know that the payment will be used
to make contributions or expenditures. Government Code Section
82015, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215.

ANALYSIS

The term "contribution" is defined in Government Code
Section 82015 to include "a payment ... except to the extent
that full and adequate consideration is received unless it is
clear from the surrounding circumstances that [the payment] is
not made for political purposes." We have adopted a regulation
further interpreting the term "contribution," 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18215, which provides that all receipts of candidates,
controlled committees, official committees of a political party,
and organizations the major purpose of which is the election or
defeat of candidates and measures should be characterized as
"contributions."

The Democratic Clubs are not official committees of the
Democratic Party because they are not part of the state central
committee and the county central committees that make up the
official structure of the Democratic Party in California. More-
over, they are not organized for the major purpose of electing or
defeating candidates and ballot measures. Rather, they exist to
provide a forum for discussing issues, a place to socialize and a
means to perform community services, as well as a mechanism for
supporting Democratic candidates.

Under these circumstances, receipts of the Democratic
Clubs are contributions only if they are "'earmarked' for the
making of contributions or expenditures," that is, if the donor
knows or has reason to know that the dues will be used to make
expenditures or contributions. 2 Cal. Adm. Code 18215(d). If a
club customarily uses dues to make donations to candidates and
ballot measures, or to carry on campaign activities supporting or
opposing candidates and measures, the dues paying member will be
charged with knowledge that all or part of the dues are "contri-
butions." In such a case, the club should determine the per-
centage of dues receipts that is used for political purposes, and
should characterize that portion as contributions received.

1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

No. 75-188
Page Three

However, it has been stipulated in the present opinion
request that club dues seldom are used to make "contributions" or
"expenditures." If a club decides to support certain candidates,
the club usually carries on a fund raising activity specifically
for that purpose. Dues receipts typically are spent to publish
the club newsletter and to pay for club meetings. Other payments
are made to the California Democratic Council, county central
committee and local club federations. Except for the amount paid
to the county central committee, none of these disbursements are
contributions or expenditures. Payments to a county central
committee are contributions to that organization because the
county central committee is an official committee of the Democratic Party and, accordingly, all payments to that organization
are "contributions" to the committee. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18215(b).

If dues receipts routinely are paid to the county central committee, those receipts should be recorded as contributions received by the club. If the amount of such contributions
plus funds generated by specific fund raising activities in support of candidates and measures aggregate $500 in a calendar
year, the club will become a "committee," as defined in Government Code §2013(a), and will have to file periodic reports, as required by Chapter 4, Sections 84100, et seq. of the
Political Reform Act. However, if the club does not receive
contributions aggregating $500 in a calendar year, and does not
engage in other activities which would render it a committee
within the meaning of Section 82013, it will not have any filing
obligations under the Political Reform Act.

Approved by the Commission August 3, 1976. Concurring:
Brosnahan, Carpenter, Lapan, Lowenstein and Quinn.

[signature]
Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

2/ In the Opinion requested by Katherine Rawlings, 1
FPPC Opinions 62 (No. 75-053, July 2, 1975), we concluded that
monthly payments paid to a county central committee are
contributions.

PERCY V. CONO (1927-1953)
BENT W. LEVIT
VICTOR B. LEVIT
DAVID P. HARRISON
J. HARRIS GREEN
ALLAN P. HOLTZEN
FRANCIS O. WILMARTE
BERNARD FRIEDMAN
GERALD M. LACHOWICZ
H. PAUL BRESLAU
IVAN GOULD
ROBERT C. HUGHES
GEORGE A. JOHNSON
SANFORD I. JOHNSON
RICHARD D. WOLF
CHARLES W. DYER
HARRY M. SNYDER
NOAH R. RACTER
DAN D. LAZARJ
WILLIAM M. JACKSON
S. DAVID ROOS
BARRY ZALMA
IRVING G. BREYER
LLOYD E. OMAYBIEL
ALAN STAMM
BEVL GANN

LOS ANGELES
1900 AVENUE OF THE STARS
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90067
(213) 879-1222

LAW OFFICES

LONG & LEVIT
465 CALIFORNIA STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104
TELEPHONE (415) 397-2222

12 December 1975

Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento CA 95814

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-188

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

I am writing you further concerning my letter
of 16 October 1975 concerning dues payments to Democratic
Clubs, and in response to your letter of 4 November 1975.

If possible, I would appreciate receiving the
draft of the opinion before the end of the year. Our
political clubs will be receiving dues shortly, and I am
getting many requests as to how they should be handled.

I would also like to confirm some of the state-
ments I gave to Natalie West of your staff on 28 October
1975.

I told Ms. West that the California Democratic
Counsel (with which many of the Democratic Clubs affiliate)
has in the past been a political "Committee" and will
probably be one in the future. To affiliate, each club
pays the CDC a fee of $10. In addition, each club must
affiliate at least 20 of its members as members of the CDC.
To do this, it sends the CDC the name and address of the
member, and $2.00 for each member. Therefore, although
payments to the CDC by clubs are usually made by one check,
that check represents the affiliation of the club ($10.00)
and of each listed member ($2.00 each).

Page 2


Finally, I would appreciate receipt of copies of all forms and manuals promulgated by the Fair Political
Practices Commission, together with an address where clubs
can order such forms and manuals.

Thank you for your assistance.

Yours very truly,

[signature]
Francis Willmarth

FW:srd
cc: Wallace Albertson
    Adria Garabedian
    Ed Munak
    John Harden

PERCY V LONG (1927-1953)
BENT N LEVIT
VICTOR B LEVIT
B C PATTERSON
BERNARD LIND
ALAN P GARFIELD
ALLEN G WILLMARTH
BERNARD BALLEN
SAMUEL B SCHWARTZ
WALTER S.WEISS
KEITH R HUGHES
JOSEPH KAUS
RICHARD B.WOLF
H PAUL RHETT
JOHN B.STEVENS
SANDER L JOHNSON
INGRID NEUSTAETTER
JAY FENTON
WILLIAM M JACKSON
LOIS O.ANDREWS
HARVEY BERGER
IRVING O BRICKER
LLOYD E GRAYEIEL
ALAN STAMM
SPECIAL COUNSEL

LOS ANGELES
1902 AVENUE OF THE STARS
LOS ANGELES,CALIFORNIA 90067
(213) 879-1222

LAW OFFICES

LONG & LEVIT
465 CALIFORNIA STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104

TELEPHONE (415) 397-2222

16 October 1975

Request No._____________

Date Received 10-20-75

Response Due____________
11-3-75

Fair Political Practices
Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca 95814

Gentlemen:

I am legal counsel for California Democratic
Council, a California non-profit corporation. Its members
consist of, among others, Democratic Clubs throughout the
State.

Individual Democrats who join Democratic Clubs
customarily pay dues to these clubs, normally at the beginning of the year. These dues normally range between
$3.00 and $6.00 a person. At the time the club collects
these dues, it normally does not know the use to which
they will be put. A substantial portion ($2.00 per
affiliated member) goes to the California Democratic Council,
to affiliate the individual member with the CDC. Other
amounts are paid to county central committees, and to local
club federations. Another substantial portion goes to send
out the regular club newsletters to members. Other amounts
are spent on putting on club meetings, at which the clubs
normally have programs on political and social matters of
current interest.

In my experience, little or none of the dues money
is left to make contributions to or to support political
candidates or ballot measures, and initially the dues are
not collected for the purpose of making such contributions.
Instead, if a club decides to support a candidate or ballot
measure, its normal practice is to have members participate

75 138

Fair Political Practices                    16 October 1975
Commission                               2

in fund raising efforts put on by other organizations, or to
hold special fund raising efforts to raise money for the candi-
dates or ballot measures.

Neither the CDC or its member clubs are committees
or subcommittees of the Democratic Party.

In light of the foregoing facts, we hereby request
an opinion under Government Code Section 83114 as to whether
these dues are to be counted in determining whether a club
has received sufficient contributions or made sufficient ex-
penditures to make it a "committee". It is our opinion that
they are not.

The term "committee" is defined in Government Code
Section 82013 to mean "...any person or combination of persons
who directly or indirectly receives contributions or makes
expenditures or contributions for the purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or
against the nomination or election of one or more candidates,
or the passage or defeat of any measure", if the contributions
or expenditures exceed certain maximum amounts. [Emphasis
added]

In the present case, club dues are paid by the
members in order to become members of the club, and to support
the club in its general functions, and are not made to influence
the election of a candidate or a ballot measure. Since they
are not made for such purposes, and are rarely so used, they
should not be counted in determining whether the club has had
sufficient contributions or expenses to be a "committee".

We corresponded with the Secretary of State's Office
in 1974 on this issue, under the prior law (Waxman-Dymally),
and they agreed with this interpretation.

If, of course, the club engages in a fund raiser in
order to raise funds for a particular candidate or ballot measure,
or for campaign purposes in general, we agree that these funds

Fair Political Practices
Commission                    3                 16 October 1975

would clearly be "contributions" referred to in Government
Code Section 82013.

     Please let me hear from you.

                                        Yours very truly,

                                        [signature]
                                        Francis Wilmarth
FGW:gea

sections.conclusion: Dues received by the Democratic Clubs are not contributions, unless the dues are "'earmarked' for the making of contributions or expenditures." A payment is "earmarked" when the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment will be used to make contributions or expenditures. Government Code Section 82015, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215.
sections.analysis:
The term "contribution" is defined in Government Code Section 82015-1/ to include "a payment ... except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that [the payment] is not made for political purposes." We have adopted a regulation further interpreting the term "contribution," 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215, which provides that all receipts of candidates, controlled committees, official committees of a political party, and organizations the major purpose of which is the election or defeat of candidates and measures should be characterized as "contributions."

The Democratic Clubs are not official committees of the Democratic Party because they are not part of the state central committee and the county central committees that make up the official structure of the Democratic Party in California. Moreover, they are not organized for the major purpose of electing or defeating candidates and ballot measures. Rather, they exist to provide a forum for discussing issues, a place to socialize and a means to perform community services, as well as a mechanism for supporting Democratic candidates.

Under these circumstances, receipts of the Democratic Clubs are contributions only if they are "'earmarked' for the making of contributions or expenditures," that is, if the donor knows or has reason to know that the dues will be used to make expenditures or contributions. 2 Cal. Adm. Code 18215(d). If a club customarily uses dues to make donations to candidates and ballot measures, or to carry on campaign activities supporting or opposing candidates and measures, the dues paying member will be charged with knowledge that all or part of the dues are "contributions." In such a case, the club should determine the percentage of dues receipts that is used for political purposes, and should characterize that portion as contributions received.

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

However, it has been stipulated in the present opinion request that club dues seldom are used to make "contributions" or "expenditures." If a club decides to support certain candidates, the club usually carries on a fund raising activity specifically for that purpose. Dues receipts typically are spent to publish the club newsletter and to pay for club meetings. Other payments are made to the California Democratic Council, county central committee and local club federations. Except for the amount paid to the county central committee, none of these disbursements are contributions or expenditures. Payments to a county central committee are contributions to that organization because the county central committee is an official committee of the Democratic Party and, accordingly, all payments to that organization are "contributions" to the committee. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(b).2/

If dues receipts routinely are paid to the county central committee, those receipts should be recorded as contributions received by the club. If the amount of such contributions, plus funds generated by specific fund raising activities in support of candidates and measures aggregate $500 in a calendar year, the club will become a "committee," as defined in Government Code Section 82013(a), and will have to file periodic reports, as required by Chapter 4, Sections 84100, et seq. of the Political Reform Act. However, if the club does not receive contributions aggregating $500 in a calendar year, and does not engage in other activities which would render it a committee within the meaning of Section 82013, it will not have any filing obligations under the Political Reform Act.

Approved by the Commission August 3, 1976. Concurring: Brosnahan, Carpenter, Lapan, Lowenstein and Quinn.

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

2/ In the Opinion requested by Katherine Rawlings, 1 FPPC Opinions 62 (No. 75-053, July 2, 1975), we concluded that monthly payments paid to a county central committee are contributions.

Fair Political Practices Commission

16 October 1975

would clearly be "contributions" referred to in Government Code Section 32013.

Please let me hear from you.

Yours very truly,

Francis Willmarth

embedding.qa_text: CONCLUSION: Dues received by the Democratic Clubs are not contributions, unless the dues are "'earmarked' for the making of contributions or expenditures." A payment is "earmarked" when the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment will be used to make contributions or expenditures. Government Code Section 82015, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215.
